panel.origin: A precise, step-by-step set of instructions for solving a problem or completing a task. The fundamental recipe behind every program.
panel.mixology: She optimized the search algorithm to find results in milliseconds instead of seconds.
panel.examples: From the name of 9th-century Persian mathematician al-Khwarizmi, Latinized as 'Algoritmi' — whose work on systematic calculation gave us the term.

panel.origin: A reusable, self-contained block of code that performs a specific task and can be called whenever needed. The basic unit of organizing code.
panel.mixology: Instead of repeating the same logic five times, he wrote one function and called it where needed.
panel.examples: Borrowed from mathematics, where a function maps inputs to outputs — programming functions do the same with data, taking arguments and returning results.

panel.origin: A named container that stores a value which can change as a program runs. The basic way code remembers and manipulates data.
panel.mixology: He stored the user's score in a variable and updated it each time they earned points.
panel.examples: From mathematics' 'variable' (a quantity that can vary). In code it's literally a labeled box holding a value that may change over time.

panel.origin: A structure that repeats a block of code multiple times — until a condition is met or for each item in a collection. How programs handle repetition.
panel.mixology: A simple loop printed every name in the list without writing the same line a hundred times.
panel.examples: From the everyday 'loop' (a circle returning on itself) — the code execution circles back to repeat until told to stop.

panel.origin: Code that makes decisions — running different instructions depending on whether a condition is true or false. The 'if this, then that' logic of programming.
panel.mixology: The conditional checked if the user was logged in, showing the dashboard if true and the login page if not.
panel.examples: From 'condition' — the code branches based on conditions. The 'if/else' statement is the most fundamental conditional in nearly every language.

panel.origin: A technique where a function calls itself to solve a problem by breaking it into smaller versions of the same problem. Powerful, elegant, and famously mind-bending.
panel.mixology: He solved the folder-scanning task with recursion: the function processed each folder by calling itself on subfolders.
panel.examples: From Latin 'recurrere' (to run back). The classic joke: 'To understand recursion, you must first understand recursion' — a function defined in terms of itself.

panel.origin: Hiding complex details behind a simpler interface, so you can use something without knowing exactly how it works inside. A core principle for managing complexity.
panel.mixology: The payment library was a clean abstraction — she called one function and never saw the messy banking details underneath.
panel.examples: From Latin 'abstrahere' (to draw away). It 'draws away' the complicated internals, leaving a simple surface — like driving a car without understanding the engine.

panel.origin: Object-Oriented Programming — a style that organizes code around 'objects' that bundle data and the actions that work on it. One of the dominant programming paradigms.
panel.mixology: Using OOP, he modeled each user as an object with its own properties and methods.
panel.examples: An acronym for a paradigm popularized in the 1980s–90s; it organizes programs around 'objects' rather than just sequences of instructions.

panel.origin: An ordered collection of values stored in a single variable, accessed by their position (index). One of the most fundamental data structures.
panel.mixology: She stored the seven days in an array and grabbed any one by its index number.
panel.examples: From the everyday 'array' (an ordered arrangement). In most languages the index starts at zero — a famous source of off-by-one confusion.

panel.origin: A data structure that stores values as key-value pairs (also called a dictionary, map, or hash). You look up values by name rather than position.
panel.mixology: He used an object to store the user's profile, fetching the email by its key instead of a number.
panel.examples: The 'object/dictionary' name fits: like a dictionary, you look up a value (definition) by its key (word) rather than by position.

panel.origin: A sequence of characters — text. From a single letter to an entire document, any text data is a string.
panel.mixology: The function took a string of the user's name and capitalized the first letter.
panel.examples: From the image of characters 'strung' together in sequence, like beads on a thread — one of the oldest data-type names in computing.

panel.origin: A data type with only two possible values: true or false. The foundation of all logic and decision-making in code.
panel.mixology: A boolean flag tracked whether the form was valid, flipping to true once all fields passed.
panel.examples: Named after 19th-century mathematician George Boole, whose 'Boolean algebra' of true/false logic became the bedrock of digital computing.

panel.origin: Two ways to organize data by order: a 'stack' is last-in-first-out (like a pile of plates); a 'queue' is first-in-first-out (like a line at a store).
panel.mixology: He used a stack to track the undo history and a queue to process incoming jobs in order.
panel.examples: 'Stack' from a literal pile (you take from the top); 'queue' from the British word for a waiting line (first to arrive is first served).

panel.origin: Application Programming Interface — a defined way for two pieces of software to talk to each other. How apps request data and services from one another.
panel.mixology: The weather app pulled live forecasts by calling a third-party API instead of measuring the sky itself.
panel.examples: An acronym describing the 'interface' through which programs interact — the contract specifying how to ask for something and what you'll get back.

panel.origin: JavaScript Object Notation — a lightweight, human-readable text format for storing and exchanging data. The lingua franca of web APIs.
panel.mixology: The API returned the user's data as JSON, which the app parsed into usable objects.
panel.examples: An acronym from its JavaScript origins; its simple, readable structure of keys and values made it the dominant data-exchange format on the web.

panel.origin: An organized collection of data stored so it can be efficiently searched, updated, and managed. The persistent memory behind almost every app.
panel.mixology: Every order was saved to the database, so the records survived long after the app was closed.
panel.examples: From 'data' + 'base' (a foundation or store). The structured store that holds an application's information permanently, beyond a single session.

panel.origin: The dominant version-control system, which tracks every change to code over time and lets teams collaborate without overwriting each other's work.
panel.mixology: Thanks to Git, she could see exactly who changed which line, and roll back the bug to last week's version.
panel.examples: Created by Linus Torvalds (of Linux fame) in 2005; he jokingly named it after British slang for an unpleasant person — 'I name all my projects after myself.'

panel.origin: A saved snapshot of changes in version control, with a message describing what changed. The basic unit of recorded progress in Git.
panel.mixology: He made a commit with the message 'fix login bug' so the team could see exactly what he'd changed.
panel.examples: From 'commit' (to formally record or pledge) — you 'commit' your changes to the project's permanent history.

panel.origin: A separate line of development that lets you work on a feature without affecting the main code; 'merging' combines a branch back in when it's ready.
panel.mixology: She created a branch to build the new feature, then merged it into the main code once it was tested.
panel.examples: From the tree metaphor: development 'branches' off from the trunk to grow independently, then merges back — a vivid model of parallel work.

panel.origin: A proposal to merge your code changes into the main project, opened for others to review, discuss, and approve. The heart of team collaboration on platforms like GitHub.
panel.mixology: He opened a pull request, and two teammates reviewed his code before it was approved and merged.
panel.examples: Named because you're requesting that maintainers 'pull' your changes into their codebase — the standard gateway for contributing to shared projects.

panel.origin: Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment — automated pipelines that test code as it's added and push it live with minimal manual steps. Modern software delivery.
panel.mixology: Their CI/CD pipeline ran every test automatically and deployed passing code to production within minutes.
panel.examples: Acronyms from the DevOps movement; 'continuous' captures the goal of integrating and shipping code constantly rather than in big, risky batches.

panel.origin: The process of finding and fixing errors ('bugs') in code. Often the most time-consuming part of programming.
panel.mixology: He spent the afternoon debugging, tracing the crash back to a single misplaced comma.
panel.examples: From removing 'bugs' — a term famously linked to a 1947 incident when a real moth was found jamming a Harvard computer, though 'bug' for a flaw predates it.

panel.origin: Restructuring existing code to make it cleaner, simpler, or faster — without changing what it actually does. Improving the inside, not the behavior.
panel.mixology: Before adding features, she refactored the tangled function into three clear, readable pieces.
panel.examples: A compound of 're-' (again) + 'factor' (from mathematics, breaking into components) — reorganizing code's structure while preserving its output.

panel.origin: Short for 'repository' — the storage location for a project's code and its entire change history. Where a codebase lives, often on GitHub.
panel.mixology: She cloned the repo to her laptop to start working on the project locally.
panel.examples: A clipping of 'repository' (a place where things are stored), from Latin 'reponere' (to put back). Universally shortened to 'repo' among developers.

panel.origin: 'Frontend' is what users see and interact with (the interface); 'backend' is the behind-the-scenes server, logic, and database. Two halves of most web apps.
panel.mixology: The frontend showed a sleek button, while the backend quietly processed the payment and updated the database.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the 'front' the user faces versus the 'back' they never see. A 'full-stack' developer works across both.

panel.origin: A pre-built foundation of reusable code and structure that speeds up development by handling common tasks, so you don't start from scratch.
panel.mixology: Rather than build everything by hand, they used a web framework that handled routing and security out of the box.
panel.examples: From the literal 'framework' (a supporting structure). It provides the skeleton you build your specific app on top of — scaffolding for code.

panel.origin: HyperText Transfer Protocol — the rules governing how web browsers and servers communicate. 'HTTPS' is its encrypted, secure version.
panel.mixology: The browser sent an HTTP request for the page, and the server responded with the HTML.
panel.examples: From its role transferring 'hypertext' (linked documents) on the web; the 'S' in HTTPS stands for 'Secure', added once encryption became essential.

panel.origin: The 'client' (your browser or app) requests something; the 'server' (a remote computer) provides it. The basic two-party model of the internet.
panel.mixology: The client app requested today's news, and the server sent back the latest articles.
panel.examples: 'Server' from one who serves; 'client' from one who is served. The model mirrors a restaurant — you (client) order, the kitchen (server) delivers.

panel.origin: A temporary store of frequently-used data kept close at hand so it can be retrieved quickly, avoiding slower repeated work. Speeds up nearly everything.
panel.mixology: The site cached the homepage so returning visitors loaded it instantly instead of rebuilding it each time.
panel.examples: From French 'cacher' (to hide) — a hidden, fast store of data. Pronounced 'cash'. 'Clearing the cache' is the classic first fix for stale data.

panel.origin: To release code to a live environment where real users can access it — taking a project from a developer's machine to production.
panel.mixology: After testing, they deployed the update, and within minutes users worldwide saw the new feature.
panel.examples: From military usage (to position forces into action). In software, you 'deploy' code into live operation — moving it from development into the real world.

panel.origin: An external piece of code or library that your project relies on to function. Modern software is built on towering stacks of dependencies.
panel.mixology: The app wouldn't build until she installed a missing dependency the code relied on.
panel.examples: From 'depend' — your code depends on this external piece. 'Dependency hell' is the dreaded tangle when these libraries conflict with one another.

panel.origin: An error or flaw in code that makes it behave incorrectly. The universal name for things gone wrong in software.
panel.mixology: A nasty bug crashed the app whenever a user entered an emoji in their name.
panel.examples: Engineers used 'bug' for flaws long before computers; it entered computing lore via a 1947 moth literally stuck in a Harvard machine, which was taped into the logbook.

panel.origin: Standard, repetitive code that must be included in many places with little change — necessary setup that adds bulk but little unique value.
panel.mixology: Every new file started with the same twenty lines of boilerplate before the real logic began.
panel.examples: From 19th-century printing, where 'boilerplate' was pre-cast metal text syndicated to newspapers — reused, standardized blocks, exactly as in code.

panel.origin: 'Spaghetti code' is tangled, disorganized code that's hard to follow — logic jumping around with no clear structure, like a plate of noodles.
panel.mixology: The legacy module was pure spaghetti code, every change risking three new bugs elsewhere.
panel.examples: A vivid metaphor from the 1970s: the control flow is so twisted and interwoven that tracing it is like following a single strand through a plate of spaghetti.

panel.origin: 'Rubber-duck debugging' — explaining your code line by line to an inanimate object (like a rubber duck) to find the flaw yourself. The act of explaining reveals the bug.
panel.mixology: Stuck for an hour, she explained the function aloud to her desk duck and spotted the error mid-sentence.
panel.examples: From a story in a programming book where a developer carried a rubber duck and debugged by explaining code to it — the act of articulating clarifies the problem.

panel.origin: The future cost of choosing a quick, easy solution now instead of a better one that would take longer. Like financial debt, it accrues 'interest' over time.
panel.mixology: Rushing the launch piled up technical debt they spent the next year slowly paying down.
panel.examples: A metaphor coined by programmer Ward Cunningham: shortcuts taken today are a 'debt' that must eventually be repaid, with interest, in extra work.

panel.origin: To release a product or feature to users. A rallying cry in dev culture for getting work out the door rather than endlessly polishing.
panel.mixology: The feature was good enough and the deadline loomed, so the lead said 'ship it' and they launched.
panel.examples: From the sense of 'shipping' a finished product. It captures a culture valuing delivery over perfectionism — 'real artists ship', as the saying goes.
